picks.thesis: Tech-enabled health insurer taking share from legacy carriers. Q1 EPS beat 85%; medical loss ratio improved 75.4% to 70.5%. P/S ~0.5x vs peers 0.7-1.1x.
picks.rationale:
Oscar Health is doing something rare in healthcare insurance — making money on tech-enabled efficiency rather than denying claims. Fact Their medical loss ratio (the % of premiums paid out as actual care) improved from 75.4% to 70.5% year-over-year. Interp That's a 4.9-point margin gain, which is enormous in insurance. The legacy insurers (UnitedHealth, Cigna, Elevance) are still wrestling with rising MLRs.

Fact Q1 2026 EPS came in $2.07 vs $1.12 expected — an 85% beat. Net income hit a record $679M. Membership grew to 3.17M.

The valuation case: OSCR trades at ~0.5x price-to-sales versus 0.7–1.1x for legacy peers. Interp Even if it never reaches peer multiple, just closing half that gap implies meaningful upside before any earnings growth is factored in.
picks.horizon_reason: The thesis is a structural shift — tech-enabled insurance taking share from legacy insurers over years. Interp That doesn't show up in a week. The next earnings report (Q2) is ~2 months out and is the key catalyst: if MLR stays improved, the thesis is confirmed and the multiple expands. If MLR jumps back to 75%+, Q1 was a one-quarter pop, not a trend. 8 weeks gives you one full earnings cycle to find out.
picks.falsification: MLR rises above 75% in Q2, OR membership growth turns negative YoY, OR ACA enhanced premium tax credits cut without offset.

picks.thesis: Event-driven: Q1 earnings May 13 BMO. The AI infra 'first crack' confirmation test after CRWV's miss. Bull case: revenue beat + margin improvement = thesis intact.
picks.rationale:
NBIS reports Q1 earnings tomorrow (May 13, before market open). The entire AI-infrastructure cohort just got beaten up by CRWV's guidance miss. Interp Nebius is the most direct comp, so this print determines the sector's next move.

Fact Contracted backlog approaching $50B (including a $27B Meta deal and up to $19.4B from Microsoft). NVIDIA invested $2B in March. They were sold out through Q1 2026. Interp That's the strongest order book in the space.

Asymmetric setup: if Nebius prints clean — revenue beat + margin improvement — the 'AI infra is cracking' narrative gets paused for a quarter, and the entire sector re-rates higher. Spec If it misses or margins compress, the read-through to every other AI-infra name is brutal.
picks.horizon_reason: This is the textbook short-horizon trade. The entire reason to own NBIS right now is the specific date of the earnings call. Once Wednesday's call happens, the thesis is either confirmed or destroyed — there's no 'wait and see for 8 weeks.' Interp 2 weeks gives you the earnings reaction plus a few days for the dust to settle.
picks.falsification: Revenue misses $316.9M consensus, OR margin compression (EBIT% worsens vs Q4), OR forward ARR guidance reduced.

picks.thesis: The 'Great Rotation' from Issue No. 2 — small-caps outperforming mega-caps for the first time in over a decade. OBBBA tax provisions, Fed easing, and AI buildout broadening favor small-caps disproportionately.
picks.rationale:
For 18 months, capital concentrated in a handful of mega-cap tech names (the Magnificent Seven). Fact That's started to reverse — IWM (Russell 2000) is up ~8-9% YTD vs the S&P up modestly. First time in over a decade small-caps are meaningfully outperforming.

Interp Three forces driving it:

  • OBBBA tax provisions — 100% bonus depreciation and immediate R&D expensing benefit capital-intensive small-caps far more than software giants.
  • Fed easing — more-leveraged small-caps benefit disproportionately when rates come down.
  • AI buildout broadening — capital is shifting from 'narrow hardware' (chip makers) to 'real economy' beneficiaries (electrical, HVAC, construction), most of which are small-cap.

Spec The closest historical analog is 2000–2006 when small-caps outperformed the S&P by ~80% cumulative after the dot-com bubble burst. Whether 2026 follows the same path is unknown, but the structural conditions are similar.
picks.horizon_reason: Rotations are slow. They don't happen in a week. Interp The valuation gap between Russell 2000 (forward P/E ~19x) and S&P (~21x) closes over months, not days. The catalysts that accelerate it — May CPI this week, then the June FOMC meeting — set the trajectory but the actual rotation plays out over 2–4 months. 8 weeks captures the next FOMC cycle.
picks.falsification: May CPI prints >=2.8% (hot, signals Fed pause), OR IWM/SPY ratio declines for 4 consecutive weeks, OR Fed signals June pause/hike at any FOMC communication.

picks.thesis: AI compute exposure at a discount to NVDA. Q1 already beat; data center revenue accelerating on MI300/MI350 ramp. The 'profitable AI vs spending AI' framing from Issue No. 2 puts AMD on the profitable side.
picks.rationale:
AMD is the cheaper way to own the AI compute trade. NVDA gets all the attention and trades at forward ~35x earnings. Fact AMD trades at ~30x, doing similar work (selling AI accelerators to hyperscalers), with the growth gap narrowing.

Fact Q1 results beat estimates. Data center revenue accelerated. MI300/MI350 series is ramping — these are the chips that compete with NVDA's H100/H200 generation. Interp The framing from Issue No. 2 — 'profitable AI vs. spending AI' — puts AMD firmly on the profitable side, alongside NVDA, but at a discount.

Interp The trade is multiple expansion: as the market acknowledges AMD as a credible second-place finisher in AI accelerators, its multiple converges toward NVDA's. You don't need AMD to dethrone NVDA. You just need it to keep growing data center revenue at 30%+ YoY.
picks.horizon_reason: Not event-driven (no specific date to wait for) but not fully structural either. Interp The catalyst is the next earnings cycle — Q2 print roughly 6 weeks out. If AMD data center revenue continues >30% YoY growth, the multiple expands toward NVDA. If it stalls, AMD gets re-rated lower. 6 weeks covers exactly that print and the immediate market reaction.
picks.falsification: Q2 data center revenue growth <25% YoY (would signal MI series isn't gaining share), OR a hyperscaler announces NVDA-exclusive multi-year deal, OR gross margin compresses below 50% on the next print.

picks.thesis: Brent at $104, Iran war at 72 days. Market starting to price $100 oil as structural baseline, not spike. Energy stocks decoupled this week — consolidation before next leg, or rotation out (riskier read).
picks.rationale:
Fact Brent crude sits at $104, Iran-Israel-US conflict is now 72 days old, and Trump publicly rejected Iran's ceasefire response Monday. Interp That's longer than most geopolitical premium trades historically last — the market is starting to price $100 oil as the new baseline rather than a temporary spike.

Fact XLE rose 37% in Q1 2026. Individual names ran harder: APA +73%, OXY +58%. Fact But this past week, despite oil rising further on the failed ceasefire, energy stocks didn't move proportionally.

Interp That divergence has two readings: (1) tactical money rotating out of energy (bearish), or (2) consolidation before the next leg up (bullish). With the ceasefire publicly rejected and conflict extending, the bullish read wins more often than not.

The honest asymmetry: Spec A peace deal headline drops XLE 15–20% in days. You're explicitly betting on continued conflict, which means continued human suffering. This pick is the riskiest of the five and should be sized smallest if you actually trade it.
picks.horizon_reason: This is the trickiest horizon to justify. Not event-driven (no single date determines it), not structural (you're not betting on long-term energy fundamentals). It's macro-cyclical — betting that the geopolitical premium persists for another month. Interp 4 weeks is short enough to exit before the late-June OPEC+ meeting (which could shift supply dynamics), but long enough to capture the next CPI print and any escalation/de-escalation headlines.
picks.falsification: Iran-US ceasefire or framework agreement announcement, OR Brent crude below $92 for 5 trading days, OR Saudi/UAE announce major supply increase.
